revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/87376893|大教宣布]]"
revision.text:
'''大教宣布詔'''（たいきょうせんぷのみことのり）は、[[Meiji (era)|明治]]3年1月3日（[[1870|1870年]][[February 3|2月3日]]）に[[Emperor Meiji|明治天皇]]の名により出された[[Imperial Rescript|詔書]]<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。[[Emperor of Japan|天皇]]に[[Divinity|神格]]を与え、[[Shinto|神道]]を[[State religion|国教]]と定めて、日本（[[Empire of Japan|大日本帝国]]）を「[[Unity of ritual and government|祭政一致]]の[[State (polity)|国家]]」とする国家方針を示した。
== 解説 == [[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]後、復古神道を奉じる[[Hirata Atsutane|平田派]]の[[Kokugaku|国学者]]を中心に祭政一致論が高まり、明治2年7月8日に[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]内に宣教使が設置され、宣教長官に[[Nakayama Tadayasu|中山忠能]]・同次官に福羽美静が任命された。福羽は神祇官が[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]と改められた後には事実上の最高責任者である[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇大輔]]を務めている。
続いて、時の[[Emperor Meiji|明治天皇]]の名によりこの詔書が出され、「治教を明らかにして惟神の道を宣揚すべし」という理念が打ち出された。直接的には[[Christianity|キリスト教]]を排撃し、宣教使による神道振興と国家的保護を打ち出している。
だが、[[Haibutsu kishaku|廃仏毀釈]]による混乱や未だ地方政府としての機能を有していた[[Han system|藩]]の[[Confucianism|儒教]]・[[Buddhism|仏教]]重視理念との対立、神祇省内部の国学者間の路線対立、更に{{Interlanguage link|Japanese Westernism|ja|欧米}}からのキリスト教弾圧停止要求も重なって{{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}}の動きは不振が続き、明治5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）の{{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}}設置と[[Miyanaka ritual|宮中祭祀]]の切り離し、宣教使の廃止によって大教宣布は見直しを迫られることとなり、大教宣布の路線の再建・強化を目指した[[Daikyoin|大教院]]が翌年に設置されることとなる。
== 関連項目 ==
* 皇道宣布運動 * [[Akihito|第125代天皇明仁]] - 在位期間：[[1989|1989年]][[January 7|1月7日]] - [[2019|2019年]][[April 30|4月30日]] ** 御称号「継宮」の「継」と御名の「明」は「大教宣布」から採用された。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The '''Taikyōsenpū Minokotori''' (Imperial Rescript) was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。[The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]][State (polity)|state]".
== 解説 == [[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]後、復古神道を奉じる[[Hirata Atsutane|平田派]]の[[Kokugaku|国学者]]を中心に祭政一致論が高まり、明治2年7月8日に[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]内に宣教使が設置され、宣教長官に[[Nakayama Tadayasu|中山忠能]]・同次官に福羽美静が任命された。福羽は神祇官が[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]と改められた後には事実上の最高責任者である[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇大輔]]を務めている。
続いて、時の[[Emperor Meiji|明治天皇]]の名によりこの詔書が出され、「治教を明らかにして惟神の道を宣揚すべし」という理念が打ち出された。直接的には[[Christianity|キリスト教]]を排撃し、宣教使による神道振興と国家的保護を打ち出している。
だが、[[Haibutsu kishaku|廃仏毀釈]]による混乱や未だ地方政府としての機能を有していた[[Han system|藩]]の[[Confucianism|儒教]]・[[Buddhism|仏教]]重視理念との対立、神祇省内部の国学者間の路線対立、更に{{Interlanguage link|Japanese Westernism|ja|欧米}}からのキリスト教弾圧停止要求も重なって{{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}}の動きは不振が続き、明治5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）の{{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}}設置と[[Miyanaka ritual|宮中祭祀]]の切り離し、宣教使の廃止によって大教宣布は見直しを迫られることとなり、大教宣布の路線の再建・強化を目指した[[Daikyoin|大教院]]が翌年に設置されることとなる。
== 関連項目 ==
* 皇道宣布運動 * [[Akihito|第125代天皇明仁]] - 在位期間：[[1989|1989年]][[January 7|1月7日]] - [[2019|2019年]][[April 30|4月30日]] ** 御称号「継宮」の「継」と御名の「明」は「大教宣布」から採用された。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The '''Taikyōsenpū Minokotori''' (Imperial Rescript) was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。[The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]][[State (polity)|state]]".
== 解説 == [[Meiji Restoration|明治維新]]後、復古神道を奉じる[[Hirata Atsutane|平田派]]の[[Kokugaku|国学者]]を中心に祭政一致論が高まり、明治2年7月8日に[[Department of Divinities|神祇官]]内に宣教使が設置され、宣教長官に[[Nakayama Tadayasu|中山忠能]]・同次官に福羽美静が任命された。福羽は神祇官が[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]と改められた後には事実上の最高責任者である[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇大輔]]を務めている。
続いて、時の[[Emperor Meiji|明治天皇]]の名によりこの詔書が出され、「治教を明らかにして惟神の道を宣揚すべし」という理念が打ち出された。直接的には[[Christianity|キリスト教]]を排撃し、宣教使による神道振興と国家的保護を打ち出している。
だが、[[Haibutsu kishaku|廃仏毀釈]]による混乱や未だ地方政府としての機能を有していた[[Han system|藩]]の[[Confucianism|儒教]]・[[Buddhism|仏教]]重視理念との対立、神祇省内部の国学者間の路線対立、更に{{Interlanguage link|Japanese Westernism|ja|欧米}}からのキリスト教弾圧停止要求も重なって{{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}}の動きは不振が続き、明治5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）の{{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}}設置と[[Miyanaka ritual|宮中祭祀]]の切り離し、宣教使の廃止によって大教宣布は見直しを迫られることとなり、大教宣布の路線の再建・強化を目指した[[Daikyoin|大教院]]が翌年に設置されることとなる。
== 関連項目 ==
* 皇道宣布運動 * [[Akihito|第125代天皇明仁]] - 在位期間：[[1989|1989年]][[January 7|1月7日]] - [[2019|2019年]][[April 30|4月30日]] ** 御称号「継宮」の「継」と御名の「明」は「大教宣布」から採用された。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The '''Taikyōsenpū Minokotori''' (Imperial Rescript) was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。[The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]][[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shintoism, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu|Nakayama Tadanoh]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity|Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shintoism and national protection through missionary missions.
だが、[[Haibutsu kishaku|廃仏毀釈]]による混乱や未だ地方政府としての機能を有していた[[Han system|藩]]の[[Confucianism|儒教]]・[[Buddhism|仏教]]重視理念との対立、神祇省内部の国学者間の路線対立、更に{{Interlanguage link|Japanese Westernism|ja|欧米}}からのキリスト教弾圧停止要求も重なって{{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}}の動きは不振が続き、明治5年3月14日（[[1872|1872年]][[April 21|4月21日]]）の{{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}}設置と[[Miyanaka ritual|宮中祭祀]]の切り離し、宣教使の廃止によって大教宣布は見直しを迫られることとなり、大教宣布の路線の再建・強化を目指した[[Daikyoin|大教院]]が翌年に設置されることとなる。
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|Kishaku]], the conflict with the [[Confucianism|Confucianism]]/[[Buddhism]]-oriented philosophy of the [[Han system|Han]], which still functioned as a local government, and the conflicts among the national scholars within the Ministry of Shintoism, as well as the demand from {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization {The movement for the nationalization of Shintoism continued to be sluggish, and on March 14, 1872 ([[March 14, 1872|March 14, 1872]]), the [[Shinto nationalization]] movement was dissolved and the [[Shinto nationalization]] movement was dissolved. The establishment of the {{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|en|Ministry of Education}} on March 14, 1872 ([[1872|1872]] [[April 21|April 21]]), the separation of the [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]] The establishment of the [[Daikyoin]] in the following year was aimed at rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== 関連項目 ==
* 皇道宣布運動 * [[Akihito|第125代天皇明仁]] - 在位期間：[[1989|1989年]][[January 7|1月7日]] - [[2019|2019年]][[April 30|4月30日]] ** 御称号「継宮」の「継」と御名の「明」は「大教宣布」から採用された。
== 脚注 == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The '''Taikyōsenpū Minokotori''' (Imperial Rescript) was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。[The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]][[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shintoism, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu|Nakayama Tadanoh]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity|Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shintoism and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shintoism, combined with the demand from Japanese Westernism [ja] to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} The establishment of the {{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}} on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== References == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

The Proclamation of the Great Religion was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shintoism, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu|Nakayama Tadanoh]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity|Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shintoism and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shintoism, combined with the demand from Japanese Westernism [ja] to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} The establishment of the {{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}} on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}}
The Proclamation of the Great Religion was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870|1870]] ([[February 3|February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。The [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] was given [[Divinity|Divinity]], [[Shinto|Shinto]] was designated as the [[State religion|State religion]], and Japan ([[Empire of Japan|Dainippon Empire]]) was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shintoism, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu|Nakayama Tadanoh]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity|Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shintoism and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shintoism, combined with the demand from {{Interlanguage link|Japanese Westernism|lt=Japanese Westernists|ja|欧米}} to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward {{Interlanguage link|Shinto nationalization|ja|神道国教化}} The establishment of the {{Interlanguage link|Ministry of Education (Imperial Japan)|ja|教部省}} on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== References == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}}
The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Religion'''|大教宣布|Taikyou Senpu no mikotonori}} was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870]] ([[February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[State religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shintoism, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shintoism and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shintoism, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward [[Shinto nationalization]] The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}}
The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Religion'''|大教宣布|Taikyou Senpu no mikotonori}} was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870]] ([[February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref>。It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[State religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of ritual and government]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward [[Shinto nationalization]] The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== References == <references group=""></references> [[Category:State Shinto]] [[Category:1870 in Japan]] [[Category:1870 in law]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Daikyoin]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinity]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward [[Shinto nationalization]] The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}}
{{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyou Senpu no mikotonori}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870]] ([[February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> It declared the "way of the gods" (Shinto) as the guiding principle of the state<ref name=":1" />
It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of religion and rule]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation there was a promotion of three great teachings<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>
# respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" />
== See Also ==

It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of religion and rule]] [[State (polity)|state]]".

However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Daikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation there was a promotion of three great teachings. This is still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]]<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}}
{{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870]] ([[February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> It declared the "way of the gods" ([[Shinto]]) as the guiding principle of the state<ref name=":1" />
It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of religion and rule]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Great Doctrine Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Great Doctrine Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation there was a promotion of three great teachings. This is still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]]<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>
# respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> The "Great Doctrine" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See Also ==
* [[Missionary Office]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]]
== References == <references group=""></references>

It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of religion and rule]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a missionary mission was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The missionary mission was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Shinto]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Shinto was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through missionary missions.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Shinto, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religious Education]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of missionary missions forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the Taikyoin, which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Great Doctrine Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Great Doctrine Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation there was a promotion of three great teachings. This is still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]]<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>
# respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> The "Great Doctrine" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See Also ==
* [[Missionary Office]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]]

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Great Doctrine Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Great Doctrine Mission.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} <noinclude>{{User:RMCD bot/subject notice|1=Taikyo Proclamation|2=Talk:Proclamation of the Great Doctrine#Requested move 12 March 2023}} </noinclude> {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, [[1870]] ([[February 3]]).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> It declared the "way of the gods" ([[Shinto]]) as the guiding principle of the state<ref name=":1" />
It saw the concept of [[Divinity]] be placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a "[[Unity of religion and rule]] [[State (polity)|state]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Great Doctrine Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Great Doctrine Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Great Doctrine Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Great Doctrine Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation there was a promotion of three great teachings. This is still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]]<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Inoue |first=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref>
# respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> The "Great Doctrine" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See Also ==

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, 1870 (February 3).<ref> 安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref> 『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> It declared [[Shinto]] (the "way of the gods") as the guiding principle of the state.<ref name=":1" />
The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See Also ==
* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name=":02">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name=":2" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name=":02" /><ref name=":2" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual]]s, and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, 1870 (February 3).<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref>『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> It declared [[Shinto]] (the "way of the gods") as the guiding principle of the state.<ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a" />
The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual]]s, and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See also ==
* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

revision.text:
{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, 1870 (February 3).<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref>『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> It declared [[Shinto]] (the "way of the gods") as the guiding principle of the state.<ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a" />
The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Miyanaka ritual]]s, and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See also ==
* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See also ==
* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, 1870 (February 3).<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref>『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> It declared [[Shinto]] (the "way of the gods") as the guiding principle of the state.<ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a" />
The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
== See also ==
* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

{{Short description|Proclamation by the Japanese Emperor}} {{Multiple issues| {{Primary sources|date=February 2023}} {{Citation style|date=February 2023}} }} The {{Nihongo|'''Proclamation of the Great Doctrine'''|大教宣布|Taikyō senpu}}<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: T |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_T.html#taikyo_sempu |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> was issued in the name of [[Emperor Meiji]] on January 3, 1870 (February 3).<ref>安丸良夫・宮地正人編『日本近代思想大系5 宗教と国家』431ページ</ref><ref>『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1110074/38 歴代の詔勅]』 p.66 河野省三 内閣印刷局、1940年（国立国会図書館）</ref><ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a">{{Cite web |title=Details of 1971 (Gyo-Tsu) 69 {{!}} Judgments of the Supreme Court |url=https://www.courts.go.jp/app/hanrei_en/detail?id=51 |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www.courts.go.jp}}</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" /> It declared [[Shinto]] (the "way of the gods") as the guiding principle of the state.<ref name="wwwcourtsgojp-1971a" />
The concept of [[Divinity]] was placed on the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]], and [[Shinto]] become designated as the [[state religion]] of the [[Empire of Japan|Japanese Empire]], which was designated as a state with "[[unity of religion and rule]]".
== Commentary == After the [[Meiji Restoration]], the theory of unification of ritual and government increased, centering on [[Kokugaku]] scholars of the [[Hirata Atsutane]] school, which dedicated itself to the restoration of Shinto, and on July 8, 1869, a [[Missionary Office]] was established within the [[Department of Divinities]]. The Missionary Office was established, and [[Nakayama Tadayasu]] was appointed as the missionary director and Fukuha Yoshishige as the vice-director. Fukuha served as [[Ministry of Divinities]], the de facto chief executive officer after the Ministry of Divinities was renamed [[Ministry of Shinto|Ministry of Divinities]].
This was followed by the issuance of an imperial decree in the name of the [[Emperor Meiji]] of the time, which set forth the principle of "clarifying the doctrine of jikkyo and proclaiming the way of godliness. In direct opposition to [[Christianity]], it proposed the promotion of Shinto and national protection through Missionary Offices.
However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].

* [[Kyodo Shoku]] * [[Missionary Office]] * [[Shendao Shejiao]] * [[Unity of religion and rule]]

However, the confusion caused by the [[Haibutsu kishaku|movement to abolish Buddhism]], the conflict with the [[Confucian]]-[[Buddhist]] philosophy of the clans that still functioned as local governments, and the conflicts among national scholars within the Ministry of Divinities, combined with the demand from the west to stop the suppression of Christianity, led to the continued sluggish movement toward Shinto nationalization The establishment of the [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] on March 14, 1872 (April 21, 1872), the separation of [[Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace|Miyanaka rituals]], and the abolition of [[Missionary Office]] forced a review of the Taikyo Mission, and the [[Great Teaching Institute|Taikyo Institute]], which aimed to rebuild and strengthen the line of the Taikyo Mission, was established the following year. The following year, the [[Daikyoin|Taikyo Institute]] was established with the aim of rebuilding and strengthening the line of the Taikyo Mission.
== Three Great Teachings == In the proclamation, there was a promotion of three great teachings:
# Respect for the gods, love of country;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Taikyo |url=http://www.philtar.ac.uk/encyclopedia/shinto/taikyo.html |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=www.philtar.ac.uk}}</ref><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Making clear the principles of Heaven and the Way of Man;<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> # Reverence for the emperor and obedience to the will of the court.<ref name="wwwphiltaracuk-2023a" /><ref name="inoue-2002a" /> These are still kept by [[Shintō Taikyō]].<ref name="inoue-2002a">{{Cite journal |last1=Inoue |first1=Nobutaka |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |date=2002 |title=The Formation of Sect Shinto in Modernizing Japan |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233729 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=29 |issue=3/4 |pages=405–427 |jstor=30233729 |issn=0304-1042}}</ref> The "Taikyo" is the same as the "Great Teachings" of the [[Great Teaching Institute]], and the Taikyo of [[Shintō Taikyō]].
